data.meaning_mnemonic: You <reading>chort</reading>le (<ja>ちょっと</ja>) <vocabulary>a little</vocabulary> at a silly joke. You can’t help chortling <vocabulary>for a moment</vocabulary>. Go ahead, chortle just a little, for just a moment, to help you remember this word.

context_sentences.en: Oh, wait a moment!
context_sentences.ja: あ、ちょっとまって！

context_sentences.en: I think it's a little different.
context_sentences.ja: ちょっとちがう気がする。

context_sentences.en: I'll think about it a little.
context_sentences.ja: ちょっと考えてみるよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
From <reading>Ohio</reading> (<ja>おはよう</ja>), you shout <vocabulary>good morning</vocabulary>. Why Ohio? Because that's where you woke up this morning. Imagine yourself waking up in Ohio, and wishing everyone "good morning" at the top of your lungs.

<ja>おはよう</ja> is a casual way to say "good morning." It can also be written in kanji, but hiragana is much more common.

context_sentences.en: Good morning, Miho-chan!
context_sentences.ja: ミホちゃん、おはよう！

context_sentences.en: I texted "good morning" to Mike.
context_sentences.ja: マイクに「おはよう」とメールをした。

context_sentences.en: Ugh, I'm sleepy... morning.
context_sentences.ja: うーっ、ねむいー、おはよう。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>ホテル</ja> comes from the English word <vocabulary>hotel</vocabulary> and the meaning is exactly the same!

The only thing to watch out for is that the "l" on the end becomes <ja>ル</ja>, because that's the closest sound in Japanese.

context_sentences.en: Did you book a hotel already?
context_sentences.ja: ホテル、もうとった？

context_sentences.en: I'm just about to check out of the hotel.
context_sentences.ja: 今、ホテルをチェックアウトするところ。

context_sentences.en: Which hotel will you stay at?
context_sentences.ja: どこのホテルにステイするの？

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Colle</reading>ct (<ja>これ</ja>) <vocabulary>this one</vocabulary> right here! Go ahead, collect this one.

You can use <ja>これ</ja> to talk about anything that's close to you. Things that are close to you are easy to collect, after all!

context_sentences.en: This one is Mt Fuji.
context_sentences.ja: これがふじ山です。

context_sentences.en: This is a pen.
context_sentences.ja: これはペンです。

context_sentences.en: Give me two of these, please.
context_sentences.ja: これを二つ下さい。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
It's only <reading>through</reading> (<ja>する</ja>) doing things that we get better at them, so you need <vocabulary>to do</vocabulary> them as much as you can.

<ja>する</ja> basically means <vocabulary>to do</vocabulary>, but it’s incredibly versatile and goes beyond its English equivalent. Besides being used alone, it can be put together with a whole range of nouns to turn them into verbs. We call these <ja>する</ja> verbs and you'll learn a lot of them as we go along!

context_sentences.en: What do you want to do for Christmas?
context_sentences.ja: クリスマス、どうする？

context_sentences.en: If you don't, someone else will.
context_sentences.ja: あなたがしなくても、だれかがするよ。

context_sentences.en: Shoot there with all your might.
context_sentences.ja: そこで、力いっぱいシュートをするんだ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Ringo</reading> (<ja>リンゴ</ja>) just ate your <vocabulary>apple</vocabulary>! You were about to take a bite, and he grabbed it. What is Ringo doing snatching apples? Surely he can afford his own.

Did you know that Ringo Starr once appeared in a Japanese TV commercial for <vocabulary>apple</vocabulary> juice? That might help you remember this word, too!

<ja>リンゴ</ja> is also commonly written in hiragana, and even in kanji, but you’ll probably see it in katakana the most.

context_sentences.en: Two apple juices, please.
context_sentences.ja: リンゴジュース、二つ下さい。

context_sentences.en: Do you want an apple or a banana?
context_sentences.ja: リンゴかバナナ、いる？

context_sentences.en: This apple tastes bad!
context_sentences.ja: このリンゴ、まずい！

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>コーヒー</ja> is the katakana version of the English word <vocabulary>coffee</vocabulary>. 

Just watch out for the second syllable, which changes to <ja>ヒー</ja> in Japanese.

context_sentences.en: Do you want a cup of coffee?
context_sentences.ja: コーヒー、のむ？

context_sentences.en: Should we have a coffee break soon?
context_sentences.ja: そろそろコーヒーブレークにする？

context_sentences.en: One hot coffee, please.
context_sentences.ja: ホットコーヒー、一つ下さい。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Connie Chihua</reading>hua (<ja>こんにちは</ja>) says <vocabulary>hello</vocabulary>. Connie Chihuahua is an amazing talking chihuahua. She can only say "hello", but that alone is pretty impressive.

<ja>こんにちは</ja> is a formal way to say "hello". It’s generally used during the daytime, from late morning to early evening. Watch out for the <ja>は</ja> on the end, which is always pronounced <ja>わ</ja>.

context_sentences.en: Hello Sensei.
context_sentences.ja: 先生、こんにちは。

context_sentences.en: Hello Aya.
context_sentences.ja: アヤちゃん、こんにちは。

context_sentences.en: Hello, I'm Jenny.
context_sentences.ja: こんにちは、ジェニーです。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
He <reading>eats</reading> (<ja>いつ</ja>) and eats and eats. <vocabulary>When</vocabulary> does this guy not eat?

<ja>いつ</ja> is a question word that is used to ask <vocabulary>when</vocabulary>.

context_sentences.en: When will you do it?
context_sentences.ja: いつするの？

context_sentences.en: Until when are you in the U.S.?
context_sentences.ja: いつまでアメリカ？

context_sentences.en: From when will you be in Canada?
context_sentences.ja: いつからカナダ？

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Do re</reading> (<ja>どれ</ja>) mi fa sol la ti... <vocabulary>which one</vocabulary> of these notes are you going to start singing from?

The question word <ja>どれ</ja> is used to ask <vocabulary>which one</vocabulary> out of three or more choices.

context_sentences.en: Which one of these ties shall I give as a gift?
context_sentences.ja: プレゼントのネクタイ、どれにする？

context_sentences.en: Can you tell which ones are tasty onions?
context_sentences.ja: どれがおいしい玉ねぎか、分かる？

context_sentences.en: Hmm, which one should I cut first?
context_sentences.ja: えっと、どれから切ればいい？

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>テレビ</ja> comes from the English word <vocabulary>television</vocabulary>. It’s been shortened, which is really common with loanwords in Japanese. The "vi" sound becomes <ja>ビ</ja>, too.

<ja>テレビ</ja> is sometimes written as "TV", but even then it’s usually pronounced <ja>テレビ</ja>.

context_sentences.en: I'm going to speak on TV now.
context_sentences.ja: 今から、テレビでしゃべるんだ。

context_sentences.en: What, you're going on TV?
context_sentences.ja: えっ、テレビに出るの？

context_sentences.en: I'll turn on the TV immediately!
context_sentences.ja: すぐにテレビをつけるよ！

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Wanna play <reading>Un</reading>o (<ja>うん</ja>)? <vocabulary>Yep</vocabulary>! 'Course I do. I always wanna play Uno.

<ja>うん</ja> is a casual way to say <vocabulary>yes</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>yeah</vocabulary>, so you’d generally use it with friends and family, and in other informal situations.

context_sentences.en: Yep, let's do that!
context_sentences.ja: うん、そうしよう！

context_sentences.en: Yeah, sorry, that's wrong.
context_sentences.ja: うん、ごめん、ちがう。

context_sentences.en: Yep, it's yummy!
context_sentences.ja: うん、おいしい！

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Want me to write a <reading>hai</reading>ku (<ja>はい</ja>)? <vocabulary>Yes</vocabulary>, of course!

<ja>はい</ja> is a formal word for "yes". You can use it to let someone know they’re right, or simply that you’re listening, as well as to respond if someone says your name.

context_sentences.en: "Mr. Chandler." "Yes!"
context_sentences.ja: 「チャンドラーさん」「はい！」

context_sentences.en: Yes, thank you very much.
context_sentences.ja: はい、ありがとうございます。

context_sentences.en: Yes, understood.
context_sentences.ja: はい、分かりました。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>ノート</ja> is a shortened form of <vocabulary>notebook</vocabulary>, with an extra "o" on the end since you can’t end a word with "t" in Japanese.

You can also use this to mean <vocabulary>notes</vocabulary>, in the context of taking down notes in a class.

context_sentences.en: If it's that notebook you mean, it's on my desk.
context_sentences.ja: そのノートなら、デスクの上だよ。

context_sentences.en: Did you take good notes on the lesson?
context_sentences.ja: レッスン、ちゃんとノートにまとめた？

context_sentences.en: I need an A4 size notebook, you know.
context_sentences.ja: A4サイズのノートがいるんだよね。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
I <reading>saw your Nara</reading> (<ja>さようなら</ja>), but I then had to say <vocabulary>goodbye</vocabulary>. It was just a short trip, but at least I saw your Nara.

<ja>さようなら</ja> is a formal way to say goodbye, so a good word to use with colleagues and teachers.

A quick tip before I say goodbye: <ja>さようなら</ja> often drops its middle <ja>う</ja>, becoming <ja>さよなら</ja>.

context_sentences.en: Goodbye Sensei.
context_sentences.ja: 先生、さようなら。

context_sentences.en: Goodbye January. Hello February.
context_sentences.ja: さようなら、一月。こんにちは、二月。

context_sentences.en: So long, and thanks for all the fish.
context_sentences.ja: さようなら、今までサカナをありがとう。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Con ban, wah!</reading> (<ja>こんばんは</ja>) cries your friend, just as you say <vocabulary>good evening</vocabulary>. There's a report on the evening news about a con ban (yes, that's a ban on conning people). Wah! Exciting stuff.

You can use <ja>こんばんは</ja> as a greeting from around 5pm or 6pm, in both formal and informal situations.　Watch out for that <ja>は</ja> on the end, which is pronounced <ja>わ</ja> here too.

context_sentences.en: Oh, good evening.
context_sentences.ja: あ、こんばんは。

context_sentences.en: Good evening, everyone.
context_sentences.ja: みなさん、こんばんは。

context_sentences.en: Good evening. Nice work today!
context_sentences.ja: こんばんは〜、おつかれさまです！

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Sorry</reading> (<ja>それ</ja>), can you grab <vocabulary>that one</vocabulary> for me? Yeah, <vocabulary>that</vocabulary>, thanks!

You can use <ja>それ</ja> for things that are close to the person you're talking to, or fairly close to both of you.

context_sentences.en: That looks fun!
context_sentences.ja: それ、おもしろそうだね！

context_sentences.en: Oh, you're going to go for that?
context_sentences.ja: え、それにするの？

context_sentences.en: That's not candy, that's an eyeball.
context_sentences.ja: それはキャンディじゃなくて目玉です。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>コンビニ</ja> comes from the English word <vocabulary>convenience store</vocabulary>, but who has time for all those syllables? That's why they cut it down to "conveni". So much more convenient that way.

Watch out for the “v” sound, which usually becomes “b” in Japanese, and this word is no exception.

context_sentences.en: Oh, you work part-time at a convenience store?
context_sentences.ja: え、コンビニでバイトしてるの？

context_sentences.en: Convenience store rice balls are so good!
context_sentences.ja: コンビニのおにぎりっておいしいよね！

context_sentences.en: Oh, there's a convenience store over there!
context_sentences.ja: あ、あそこにコンビニがある！

data.meaning_mnemonic: <ja>デパート</ja> comes from  <vocabulary>department store</vocabulary>. Yet another shortened loanword! There's an extra "o" sound on the end of this one, since a word can't end in "t" in Japanese.

context_sentences.en: I'm in the department store restroom right now.
context_sentences.ja: 今、デパートのトイレにいる。

context_sentences.en: They had that at the department store.
context_sentences.ja: それ、デパートにあったよ。

context_sentences.en: I saw Leo at the department store!
context_sentences.ja: レオなら、デパートで見かけたよ！

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Sue me, Ma Sen!</reading> (<ja>すみません</ja>), you yell at Ma Sen, before quickly adding "<vocabulary>Excuse me</vocabulary>…<vocabulary>I'm sorry</vocabulary>, so <vocabulary> sorry</vocabulary>!" You shouldn't have shouted at Ma Sen like that. You hope she won’t actually sue you.

You can use <ja>すみません</ja> both to apologize and to get someone’s attention. This expression is really common in Japan, and dropping it in wherever you can will help you blend right in!

context_sentences.en: Oh, excuse me! I'm sorry!
context_sentences.ja: あ、すみません！ごめんなさい！

context_sentences.en: Excuse me, where's the "Ocean Hotel"?
context_sentences.ja: すみません、オーシャンホテルってどこですか。

context_sentences.en: Excuse me, is now a good time?
context_sentences.ja: すみません、今ちょっといいですか？

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Eeh! Eh?</reading> (<ja>いいえ</ja>) <vocabulary>No</vocabulary>! You’re so surprised by whatever the question was, you can only manage "Eeh! Eh? No!" in response.

<ja>いいえ</ja> is a formal word for "no", so you can use it to answer politely in the negative.

context_sentences.en: No, it's not.
context_sentences.ja: いいえ、ちがいます。

context_sentences.en: No, you have the wrong person.
context_sentences.ja: いいえ、人ちがいです。

context_sentences.en: No, it's not England. It's Canada.
context_sentences.ja: いいえ、イギリスじゃないです。カナダです。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
"<reading>A gnat, ah!</reading> (<ja>あなた</ja>). I’m going to get <vocabulary>you</vocabulary>! Yes YOU," you shout as you swat at that pesky gnat buzzing around your head.

<ja>あなた</ja> is a lot less common than “you” in English, but you’ll come across it in adverts, online forums, and textbooks, where it’s used to address a general, anonymous audience. Some people also use it with their significant other, in which case it sounds intimate, like "darling". In casual conversations, though, it can come off as standoffish, so it's better to use someone's name or social title (like "teacher") instead.

context_sentences.en: Is this your blog?
context_sentences.ja: これ、あなたのブログですか？

context_sentences.en: What would you do in that kind of situation?
context_sentences.ja: こんなとき、あなたならどうしますか？

context_sentences.en: I miss you darling.
context_sentences.ja: あなたがいなくてさみしです。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Remember how <ja>これ</ja> means "this one"? Well, <ja>この</ja> simply means <vocabulary>this</vocabulary>. The difference is that <ja>これ</ja> is used on its own, whereas <ja>この</ja> goes before a noun. For example, <ja>この人</ja> means "this person".

Notice how words like this starting with <ja>こ</ja> are close to you? We'll be learning a few more of these, and they all follow the same pattern!

context_sentences.en: Who's this?
context_sentences.ja: この人、だれ？

context_sentences.en: Please look at this page.
context_sentences.ja: このページを見てください。

context_sentences.en: Sorry, my schedule is full on this day.
context_sentences.ja: ごめん、この日はスケジュールがいっぱいなんだ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You need <reading>a le</reading>ns (<ja>あれ</ja>)? How about <vocabulary>that one over there</vocabulary>? You’re in a camera shop picking out a new lens, and that one over there looks good.

Use <ja>あれ</ja> to refer to something that's far from both you and the person you're talking to.

context_sentences.en: Isn't that Emily over there?
context_sentences.ja: あれ、エミリーじゃない？

context_sentences.en: That one over there is my house.
context_sentences.ja: あれがオレのうちだよ。

context_sentences.en: That was terrible, to say the least.
context_sentences.ja: いくらなんでもあれはヒドいよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>ドル</ja> is the Japanese version of the English word <vocabulary>dollar</vocabulary>. 

The dollar symbol $ is also common in Japanese, and the pronunciation is still <ja>ドル</ja>.

context_sentences.en: It says this bread costs $5.
context_sentences.ja: このパン、５ドルだって。

context_sentences.en: Is that U.S. dollars? Or is it Canadian dollars?
context_sentences.ja: それって米ドル？それともカナダドル？

context_sentences.en: The dollar to yen rate finally reached 150 yen to the dollar.
context_sentences.ja: ついにドル円のレートが１ドル＝１５０円になった。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Remember how <ja>どれ</ja> means "which one"? <ja>どの</ja> has a very similar meaning: <vocabulary>which</vocabulary>. Whereas <ja>どれ</ja> is used on its own, <ja>どの</ja> is used before a noun to ask "which" out of three or more options. So for example, <ja>どの本</ja> is "which book (out of that pile)?".

Notice how question words often start with a <ja>ど</ja>? We'll be seeing more of these!

context_sentences.en: Which child is Maria?
context_sentences.ja: どの子がマリアちゃん？

context_sentences.en: I wonder which hotel I should stay at.
context_sentences.ja: どのホテルにしようかな。

context_sentences.en: When can you do it?
context_sentences.ja: どのタイミングでできますか？

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <reading>kni</reading>fe (<ja>ない</ja>) is <vocabulary>not there</vocabulary>. It's not here and it's not there. It’s missing. It's nowhere to be found, so don't bother looking for it!

context_sentences.en: I don't have any money at all.
context_sentences.ja: お金がぜんぜんない。

context_sentences.en: Do you have scissors?
context_sentences.ja: ハサミ、ない？

context_sentences.en: I don't have scissors, but I do have a cutter.
context_sentences.ja: ハサミはないけど、カッターならあるよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
If there’s a <reading>ca</reading>r <reading>ban</reading> (<ja>カバン</ja>), you’ll need a nice <vocabulary>bag</vocabulary> to carry all your stuff in. You can’t just throw it all in the car, so get yourself a nice bag instead.

<ja>カバン</ja> is used for bags that you can close and that have a fairly rigid structure, like <vocabulary>handbag</vocabulary>s and <vocabulary>backpack</vocabulary>s, but not more flimsy bags that don't close, like plastic bags and paper bags.

This word can also be written in hiragana and kanji. All versions are pretty common, but you'll probably see it in katakana the most.

context_sentences.en: Oh, I don't have my bag!
context_sentences.ja: あ、カバンがない！

context_sentences.en: That's funny. I thought I put them in my bag.
context_sentences.ja: おかしいなぁ。カバンに入れたはずなのに。

context_sentences.en: I opted for a larger bag that can hold a lot of stuff.
context_sentences.ja: いっぱい入るよう、大きめのカバンにしたよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: <ja>タクシー</ja> is the katakana version of the English word <vocabulary>taxi</vocabulary>. The "xi" sound becomes <ja>クシー</ja> to fit better with Japanese pronunciation.

context_sentences.en: I can't find a cab at all.
context_sentences.ja: タクシーがぜんぜん見つからないんだよね。

context_sentences.en: Shall we take a cab?
context_sentences.ja: タクシーで行く？

context_sentences.en: Don't use me as a taxi!
context_sentences.ja: タクシー代わりにしないでよ！

data.meaning_mnemonic: Remember how <ja>それ</ja> means "that one"? <ja>その</ja> has a very similar meaning: <vocabulary>that</vocabulary>. It's used before a noun that's either close to the person you're talking to, or fairly close to both of you. For example, <ja>その本</ja> is "that book", and the book in question might be in the hand of the person you're talking to, or it might be on a shelf not far from you both.

context_sentences.en: That person is famous.
context_sentences.ja: その人、有名人だよ。

context_sentences.en: I'm busy that day.
context_sentences.ja: その日は、いそがしいんだ。

context_sentences.en: At that moment, there was a loud boom.
context_sentences.ja: そのとき、ドーンと大きな音がした。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Remember how <ja>あれ</ja> means "that one over there"? Well, <ja>あの</ja> means <vocabulary>that</vocabulary>, and goes before a noun to point out something that is far away from both you and the person you're talking to, such as <ja>あの本</ja> (that book over there) or <ja>あの子犬</ja> (that puppy over there).

Notice how these words starting with <ja>あ</ja> are far away from you? We'll be learning a few more of these, and they all follow the same pattern! What's more, you’ll notice that words ending in <ja>の</ja> tend to go before a noun.

context_sentences.en: I think I've seen that person over there somewhere before.
context_sentences.ja: あの人、どこかで見たことある気がする。

context_sentences.en: Do you remember that day?
context_sentences.ja: あの日のこと、おぼえてる？

context_sentences.en: I should have said "thank you" at the time.
context_sentences.ja: あのとき、「ありがとう」って言えばよかった。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
I want to show you my <reading>demo</reading> (<ja>でも</ja>), <vocabulary>but</vocabulary> you don't want to see it. <vocabulary>However</vocabulary>, I'm going to show it to you anyway. So there.

Something to watch out for with <ja>でも</ja> is that it always goes at the beginning of a sentence, not in the middle, like "but" usually does in English. It’s also a fairly casual word, so it’s not normally used in formal situations.

context_sentences.en: But I can't.
context_sentences.ja: でも、できないよ。

context_sentences.en: But are you okay with that?
context_sentences.ja: でも、いいの？

context_sentences.en: But it's 50 percent off, you know?
context_sentences.ja: でも、５０パーセントオフだよ？

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You've been getting <reading>madder</reading> (<ja>まだ</ja>) and madder, and you’re <vocabulary>still</vocabulary> mad. You’re <vocabulary>not yet</vocabulary> as mad as you could be, though.

You can use <ja>まだ</ja> to talk about a situation that started in the past and continues into the present. Wait! Where are you going? I'm still explaining this! I'm not done yet!

context_sentences.en: I don't know that yet.
context_sentences.ja: それはまだ分からないよ。

context_sentences.en: What, are you still in the bathroom?
context_sentences.ja: え、まだトイレにいるの？

context_sentences.en: If you're looking for Ken, he's still taking a shower.
context_sentences.ja: ケンなら、まだシャワーしてるよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: Remember how words beginning with <ja>こ</ja> are close to you? It ends with <ja>こ</ja> too, which is a clue that we’re talking about a place. So <ja>ここ</ja> means <vocabulary>here</vocabulary>.

context_sentences.en: Let's eat here!
context_sentences.ja: ここで食べよう！

context_sentences.en: If you're looking for your glasses, they're here.
context_sentences.ja: メガネなら、ここにあるよ。

context_sentences.en: Where am I? Who am I?
context_sentences.ja: ここはどこ？私はだれ？

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Oh yah! Two</reading> (<ja>おやつ</ja>) <vocabulary>snack</vocabulary>s, just for you. Imagine your two snacks. What are they? I bet they're delicious. Oh yah!

<ja>おやつ</ja> can be anything from candy to cookies to onigiri. Basically, it's anything fairly light that’s eaten between meals.

context_sentences.en: Today I ate a muffin as a snack.
context_sentences.ja: 今日はおやつにマフィンを食べた。

context_sentences.en: Shall we take a snack break?
context_sentences.ja: そろそろおやつにする？

context_sentences.en: Kaori's snacks are always homemade!
context_sentences.ja: カオリちゃんのおやつは、いつも手作りだよ！

data.meaning_mnemonic: Remember how words beginning with <ja>ど</ja> are often question words, and words ending in <ja>こ</ja> refer to places? This one's no exception, so <ja>どこ</ja> means <vocabulary>where</vocabulary>.

context_sentences.en: Where are you going?
context_sentences.ja: どこに行くの？

context_sentences.en: Which McDonald's?
context_sentences.ja: どこのマクドナルド？

context_sentences.en: Where do you come from?
context_sentences.ja: どこから来たの？

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Woah, this place is <reading>mossy</reading>, <reading>mossy</reading> (<ja>もしもし</ja>)! This moss needs clearing up, so you call up the person in charge. <vocabulary>Hello</vocabulary>, says the person on the phone.

<ja>もしもし</ja> is a <vocabulary>telephone hello</vocabulary>, the standard way to answer the phone in Japan!

context_sentences.en: Hello, this is Yamakawa.
context_sentences.ja: もしもし、山川です。

context_sentences.en: Hello, can you hear me?
context_sentences.ja: もしもし、きこえますか？

context_sentences.en: Hello, what are you doing now?
context_sentences.ja: もしもし、今なにしてるの？

data.meaning_mnemonic: What can be eaten <reading>hot on dough</reading> (<ja>ほとんど</ja>)? <vocabulary>Most</vocabulary> things can. <vocabulary>Almost all</vocabulary> things, in fact. <vocabulary>Hardly</vocabulary> anything tastes bad when it's hot and served on dough.

context_sentences.en: I've played almost all of the games here.
context_sentences.ja: ここにあるゲーム、ほとんどプレイしたことあるよ。

context_sentences.en: I hardly understand any of this lecture.
context_sentences.ja: このレクチャー、ほとんど分からないんだけど。

context_sentences.en: Most people have smartphones nowadays.
context_sentences.ja: 今どき、スマホなんてほとんどの人がもってるでしょ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
This comes from the word <vocabulary>glass</vocabulary>, and it refers to the stuff used to make Cinderella's slippers, and windows, in which case it can also mean a <vocabulary>pane of glass</vocabulary>.

We'll learn how to say the other meanings of "glass" later!

context_sentences.en: Is this pen made of glass?
context_sentences.ja: このペン、ガラスで出来てるの？

context_sentences.en: Wow, it looks like Cinderella's glass slipper.
context_sentences.ja: うわー、シンデレラのガラスのくつみたい。

context_sentences.en: You have bird poop on your windshield.
context_sentences.ja: フロントガラスに鳥のフンが付いてるよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: Remember how words beginning with <ja>そ</ja> are either close to the person you're talking to, or fairly close to both of you? And how words ending in <ja>こ</ja> refer to places? This one's no exception. <ja>そこ</ja> means <vocabulary>there</vocabulary>.

context_sentences.en: I met my teacher there.
context_sentences.ja: そこで先生に会ったよ。

context_sentences.en: If you're looking for snacks, they're there.
context_sentences.ja: おやつなら、そこにあるよ。

context_sentences.en: You don't have to say so much!
context_sentences.ja: そこまで言わなくてもいいじゃない！

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Remember how words beginning with <ja>あ</ja> show that whatever you're talking about is far away from both you and the person you're talking to? And how words ending in <ja>こ</ja> refer to places? So what do you think this means? That right, it's <vocabulary>over there</vocabulary>.

Just watch out for the extra <ja>そ</ja> in this word. If you need help remembering this, imagine <reading>a sock oh</reading> (<ja>あそこ</ja>) so far <vocabulary>over there</vocabulary>. It's lucky that it's so far over there, because that sock stinks. You can still smell it, even though it's all the way over there in the distance.

context_sentences.en: My house is over there.
context_sentences.ja: 私のうちは、あそこだよ。

context_sentences.en: Let's take a short rest over there!
context_sentences.ja: あそこでちょっと休もう！

context_sentences.en: What's that thing over there?
context_sentences.ja: あそこにあるのは何？

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>キロ</ja> comes from <vocabulary>kilo</vocabulary>, and it can mean <vocabulary>kilogram</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>kilometer</vocabulary>, or any unit that starts with "kilo" in English.

You can also use abbreviations like "kg" and "km" in Japanese, and they're often just pronounced <ja>キロ</ja> too.

context_sentences.en: I lost five kilos in one month.
context_sentences.ja: 一ヶ月で五キロやせました。

context_sentences.en: It is roughly 3 km from our house to the school.
context_sentences.ja: うちから学校まで、大体３キロです。

context_sentences.en: This stone sells for only one yen per kilo.
context_sentences.ja: この石は、キロ当たり１円にしかなりません。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>タバコ</ja> is the Japanese version of the English word <vocabulary>tobacco</vocabulary>, and it also means <vocabulary>cigarette</vocabulary>.

Watch out for the first syllable, which is <ja>タ</ja> and not <ja>ト</ja>. <ja>タバコ</ja> can also be written in hiragana, and even in kanji, but katakana is the most common by far.

context_sentences.en: Oh, I can smell cigarette smoke.
context_sentences.ja: あ、タバコのにおいがする。

context_sentences.en: I've been struggling to quit smoking.
context_sentences.ja: タバコが中々やめられないんだ。

context_sentences.en: It's not good to smoke while walking.
context_sentences.ja: 歩きタバコはよくないよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: This is the equivalent of the English word <vocabulary>service</vocabulary>. By extension, it's often used to mean a <vocabulary>freebie</vocabulary>. Though it's generally used as a standard noun, it can also be a verbal noun used with <ja>する</ja>. In this case, it means <vocabulary>giving a freebie</vocabulary>.

context_sentences.en: The service at that restaurant isn't great, is it?
context_sentences.ja: あのレストランのサービス、ビミョーじゃない？

context_sentences.en: It's on the house.
context_sentences.ja: サービスです。

context_sentences.en: Eh, it's just lip service, isn't it?
context_sentences.ja: えー、ただのリップサービスじゃない？

context_sentences.en: When I bought cream buns, they gave me bread crusts for free.
context_sentences.ja: クリームパン買ったら、パンの耳、サービスしてくれた。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>She cashe</reading>s (<ja>しかし</ja>) in on everything. <vocabulary>However</vocabulary>, this time you're going to stop her. No more cashing in.

<ja>しかし</ja> is used at the beginning of a sentence, just like "however." It's pretty formal, and you'll see it much more in written Japanese than spoken Japanese.

context_sentences.en: However, that was a lie.
context_sentences.ja: しかし、それはウソだった。

context_sentences.en: However, it was raining that day.
context_sentences.ja: しかし、その日は雨だった。

context_sentences.en: However, she was found by the queen.
context_sentences.ja: しかし、女王に見つかってしまった。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>パン</ja> is the katakana version of the Portuguese word "pão" and means <vocabulary>bread</vocabulary> in English.

If you know the Portuguese, Spanish, or French word for bread, you shouldn't have trouble remembering this word. If not, here's a mnemonic to help you:

You use a <reading>pan</reading> (<ja>パン</ja>) to make all your <vocabulary>bread</vocabulary>. It's your special bread pan, and all the bread you make with it is delicious.

context_sentences.en: I always eat bread in the mornings.
context_sentences.ja: 朝はいつもパンを食べます。

context_sentences.en: Oh, we're out of bread.
context_sentences.ja: あ、食パン切れてる。

context_sentences.en: It says bread crusts are 100 yen.
context_sentences.ja: パンの耳、百円だって。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Look at this <reading>totem, oh</reading> (<ja>とても</ja>)! It's <vocabulary>very</vocabulary>, very, very, very lucky, as all totems should be. 

This word has a slightly formal feel to it, and you’ll come across it most in writing.

context_sentences.en: Thank you, I am very happy to hear that.
context_sentences.ja: ありがとう、とてもうれしいです。

context_sentences.en: This is a very precious teddy bear.
context_sentences.ja: これは、とても大切なテディベアなの。

context_sentences.en: That teacher's lecture is very easy to understand.
context_sentences.ja: あの先生のレクチャーはとても分かりやすい。

data.meaning_mnemonic: <ja>ビル</ja> is the Japanese version of the English word <vocabulary>building</vocabulary>. It usually refers to buildings that are taller than they are wide, most commonly apartment blocks and office buildings.

context_sentences.en: My company is in that office building over there.
context_sentences.ja: うちの会社は、あそこのオフィスビルに入ってるんだよ。

context_sentences.en: I'll call you when I get out of the building.
context_sentences.ja: ビルから出たら、電話するよ。

context_sentences.en: The moon was visible in a gap between the tall buildings.
context_sentences.ja: ビルの谷間に月が見えた。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You've <reading>mow</reading>ed your lawn (<ja>もう</ja>) <vocabulary>already</vocabulary>? Wow, you're ahead on your mowing. Good work. You'll have to do it again <vocabulary>soon</vocabulary>, though.

This can also mean <vocabulary>yet</vocabulary> in a question, and <vocabulary>anymore</vocabulary> with a negative.

context_sentences.en: I've already seen that anime.
context_sentences.ja: そのアニメ、もう見たよ。

context_sentences.en: I'm almost done!
context_sentences.ja: もうそろそろ終わる！

context_sentences.en: I understand. I won't come here anymore.
context_sentences.ja: 分かった。ここにはもう来ないね。

data.meaning_mnemonic: <ja>ワンピース</ja> comes from the English word one-piece, but it generally refers to a casual <vocabulary>dress</vocabulary> in Japanese.

context_sentences.en: That dress is so cute!
context_sentences.ja: そのワンピース、めっちゃかわいいね！

context_sentences.en: I bought five dresses at a bargain sale.
context_sentences.ja: バーゲンで、ワンピースを五着も買っちゃった。

context_sentences.en: I spilled coffee on my favorite white dress.
context_sentences.ja: お気に入りの白いワンピースにコーヒーをこぼしてしまった。

data.meaning_mnemonic: <ja>マンション</ja> comes from the English word "mansion," but it just means an ordinary <vocabulary>apartment building</vocabulary> in Japanese. Depending on context, it can also mean an individual <vocabulary>apartment</vocabulary>. Either way, it’s definitely not a big, luxurious house in Japanese!

context_sentences.en: We can see the fireworks really well from the balcony of my apartment!
context_sentences.ja: うちのマンションのベランダから、花火めっちゃよく見えるよ！

context_sentences.en: Huh, you'd rather live in an apartment than in your own house? Why?
context_sentences.ja: えーっ、マイホームよりマンションに住む方がいいの？なんで？

context_sentences.en: I'm planning to leave this apartment next month.
context_sentences.ja: 私、来月、このマンションを出る予定なんだ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>オノマトペ</ja> comes from the French word “onomatopée” which, as you can probably guess, means <vocabulary>onomatopoeia</vocabulary>.

<ja>オノマトペ</ja> has a wider meaning than its English equivalent, and it also covers <vocabulary>mimetic word</vocabulary>s, which are words that evoke ideas and actions rather than sounds.

context_sentences.en: This is an onomatopoeia that expresses a sense of softness.
context_sentences.ja: これは、やわらかいイメージを表すオノマトペですね。

context_sentences.en: What is your favorite onomatopoeia and why do you like it?
context_sentences.ja: 好きなオノマトペと、それが好きな理由を教えてください。

context_sentences.en: How do you all learn Japanese onomatopoeias?
context_sentences.ja: 日本語のオノマトペって、みんなどうやって勉強してるの？

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>トランプ</ja> comes from the English word "trump", as in "a trump card", but it means <vocabulary>deck of cards</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>playing cards</vocabulary> in Japanese.

The Japanese meaning probably comes from card games played by English-speaking Westerners back in the day. Japanese people heard the word "trump" a lot, and assumed it meant a deck of cards.

context_sentences.en: We're going to play poker, so can you go buy some playing cards?
context_sentences.ja: ポーカーするからトランプ買ってきて！

context_sentences.en: Hey, want to play cards?
context_sentences.ja: ねえ、トランプしない？

context_sentences.en: I'm not very good at shuffling cards.
context_sentences.ja: トランプ切るの、苦手なんだよね。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Who are who are</reading> (<ja>ふわふわ</ja>) <vocabulary>fluffy</vocabulary>? I’m thinking puppies, but ask yourself, "who are the fluffiest creatures I know?"

This is an <ja>オノマトペ</ja> meaning fluffy, like a puppy, a cloud, or a cotton ball. It can also describe the movement of a dandelion puffball or a jellyfish, <vocabulary>floating lightly</vocabulary> in the air or the water. And if you’re <vocabulary>feeling lightheaded</vocabulary>, that’s called <ja>ふわふわ</ja> too. Basically, anything with light or fluffy vibe!

context_sentences.en: Wow, this bread is so fluffy, isn't it!
context_sentences.ja: うわー、このパン、ふわふわだね！

context_sentences.en: Isn't it cute to see jellyfish floating in the sea?
context_sentences.ja: クラゲが海でふわふわ泳いでるのって、かわいくない？

context_sentences.en: I’m kind of light-headed.
context_sentences.ja: なんか頭がふわふわする。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Parrot, parrot</reading> (<ja>ペラペラ</ja>), your speaking is so <vocabulary>fluent</vocabulary>!

<ja>ペラペラ</ja> is an <ja>オノマトペ</ja> that describes someone speaking a language fluently, or simply being a real chatterbox.

Related to this, <ja>ペラペラ</ja> also means <vocabulary>leafing through a book</vocabulary>. And in an extension of that meaning, it can describe something <vocabulary>paper thin</vocabulary> or flimsy, like a piece of paper from that book you're leafing through.

context_sentences.en: How did you become fluent in Japanese?
context_sentences.ja: どうやって日本語ペラペラになったの？

context_sentences.en: Ah, I want to be able to speak English fluently soon.
context_sentences.ja: あー、はやく英語がペラペラ話せるようになりたい。

context_sentences.en: Hey, you've been blabbing on and on! We're in the library, remember?
context_sentences.ja: ねえ、さっきから、ペラペラペラペラうるさいよ！ここ、図書館だよ？

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Doc, Doc</reading> (<ja>ドキドキ</ja>), listen to my <vocabulary>racing heart</vocabulary>. I'm so <vocabulary>excited</vocabulary>, but I'm also a bit <vocabulary>nervous</vocabulary>.

<ja>ドキドキ</ja> is an <ja>オノマトペ</ja> used to describe the feeling of your heart beating fast. When does your heart race? When you get excited or nervous, so <ja>ドキドキ</ja> also expresses those feelings!

context_sentences.en: Oh man, I'm so nervous.
context_sentences.ja: やばい、めっちゃドキドキする。

context_sentences.en: He was so cool that my heart was pounding.
context_sentences.ja: カッコよくて、ドキドキでした。

context_sentences.en: I could feel your excitement.
context_sentences.ja: ドキドキが伝わってきたよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>バイキング</ja> comes from the English word <vocabulary>Viking</vocabulary>, but in Japanese, it often refers to a <vocabulary>buffet</vocabulary>.

<ja>バイキング</ja> came to mean "buffet" thanks to Japan’s first all-you-can-eat restaurant, "Viking", which was named after a feast scene from a movie called (you guessed it) "Viking".

context_sentences.en: Apparently, an overnight stay with a breakfast buffet costs 5,000 yen at this hotel.
context_sentences.ja: このホテル、朝食バイキング付きで五千円だって。

context_sentences.en: Would you like to go to an all-you-can-eat cake place with me sometime?
context_sentences.ja: 今度いっしょにケーキバイキングに行かない？

context_sentences.en: The Christmas party will be buffet style.
context_sentences.ja: クリスマスパーティーは、バイキング形式にする予定だよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<ja>レントゲン</ja> comes from the German word "Röntgen" meaning <vocabulary>X-ray</vocabulary>, named after their inventor Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen.

If that’s news to you, here’s a mnemonic to help you remember: 

You’re planning to <reading>rent a Gen</reading>ji (<ja>レントゲン</ja>), but you ask Genji to get an <vocabulary>X-ray</vocabulary> first, just to make sure he’s in good health before signing the rental contract.

context_sentences.en: The X-rays showed no abnormalities.
context_sentences.ja: レントゲン写真には、異常は認められませんでした。

context_sentences.en: Let's take a chest X-ray just to be sure.
context_sentences.ja: 念の為、胸部のレントゲンを撮りましょう。

context_sentences.en: Please wait in front of the X-ray room.
context_sentences.ja: レントゲン室の前でお待ちください。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Somebody tried to <reading>mug Re</reading>mi (<ja>まぐれ</ja>), your friend, but by <vocabulary>chance</vocabulary> they managed to escape! Their escape was a <vocabulary>fluke</vocabulary>, but what a relief they didn't get mugged.

This word can be written in kanji, but the kana version is way more common. In fact, it'd be quite a fluke if you saw it in kanji.

context_sentences.en: It's just a fluke.
context_sentences.ja: ただのまぐれだよ。

context_sentences.en: It was just a lucky guess.
context_sentences.ja: まぐれで当たっただけなんだよね。

context_sentences.en: Even if it's a fluke, it's still great!
context_sentences.ja: まぐれでもすごいよ！

data.meaning_mnemonic:
This word means <vocabulary>stapler</vocabulary>. It comes from the name of the company that first imported staplers into Japan: Hotchkiss! Here’s a mnemonic to help you remember it: 

Ever given a <reading>hot kiss</reading> (<ja>ホッチキス</ja>) to a <vocabulary>stapler</vocabulary>? Imagine what that feels like, and you'll remember this word forever.

context_sentences.en: Ah, we're out of staples.
context_sentences.ja: あ、ホッチキスの針、切れちゃった。

context_sentences.en: Wow, that's thick! I wonder if I can staple it together with this stapler?
context_sentences.ja: うわっ、分厚い！このホッチキスでとじれるかな？

context_sentences.en: When stapling Japanese documents, it is common practice to staple the top right corner for vertical writing and the top left corner for horizontal writing.
context_sentences.ja: 日本の書類をホッチキスで止める時は、縦書きの場合は右上、横書きの場合は左上をホチキスで止めるのが一般的です。
